heading: NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CXLIII
card_summary: This chunk consists of Saverio Campanini's scholarly endnotes (notes 253–278) to his introductory essay on Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi. The notes document the Renaissance reception of the god Pan as a symbol of universal harmony and the cosmos, tracing sources from Macrobius, Plutarch, Eusebius, Ovid, and Sannazaro's Arcadia through to Ficino, Cristoforo Landino, Francis Bacon, and Goethe. A substantial portion addresses Zorzi's use of the Tetragrammaton and the Hebrew divine name, citing Ficino's De christiana religione on the ineffable four-letter name and a medieval Christian-Jewish dialogue (in Patrologia Latina CLVII) that interprets the Tetragrammaton as simultaneously encoding Trinitarian theology. Campanini also documents Zorzi's treatment of Echo as a Pythagorean symbol of creation by divine breath (he), and the Hermetic formula of God as an intelligible sphere whose center is everywhere and circumference nowhere.
essay:
# Scholarly Summary: Notes 253–278 to Campanini's Introductory Essay

## Overview

This chunk consists entirely of Campanini's densely packed endnotes (253–278) to his introductory saggio on Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The notes collectively document the scholarly and Renaissance reception of three interrelated topics that Zorzi wove into his harmonic cosmology: (1) the allegorical figure of Pan as symbol of the universe; (2) the Tetragrammaton and the Hebrew divine name in Christian kabbalistic theology; and (3) subsidiary symbols including Echo and the Hermetic sphere. These annotations reveal the intertextual web from which Zorzi's synthesis was constructed.

## Pan as Universal Symbol: Sources and Reception History

Notes 253–270 trace the iconographic and philosophical tradition of Pan as *universitas rerum* — the All. Campanini provides a textual anchor in Sannazaro's *Arcadia* (Milan, 1509), quoting the *prosa decima* in which worshippers enter a sacred grove to find the image of "il selvatico idio" with his characteristic attributes:

> sovra la testa havea due corna drittissime et elevate verso il cielo con la faccia rubiconda come matura Fragola, le gambe e i piedi irsuti, ne d'altra forma che son quelli delle capre. Il suo manto era di una pelle grandissima stellata di bianche macchie.

<cite>Note 253, p. 143 — quoting Sannazaro, Arcadia, prosa decima (Milan, 1509)</cite>

Campanini refers readers to W.H. Roscher and K. Wernicke's still-useful article in the *Ausführliches Lexikon der griechischen und römischen Mythologie* (1902, cols. 1347–1481) and to Edgar Wind's *Pagan Mysteries in the Renaissance* (1968, pp. 191–217) for Pan's role in the Florentine Neoplatonic synthesis (note 255). The destruction of Giulio Romano's painting of Pan in the final days of World War II is noted at note 256, citing Eisler (1948).

The cosmic allegory of Pan's physical form — horns as sun and moon, starry pelt as the heavens, goat-legs as the sublunar world — is traced to Macrobius. The Pythagorean etymology deriving *Pan* from the Greek *pan* (the All) is cited from Cristoforo Landino's commentary on Virgil's *Eclogues* (Ad Ecl. 2,31):

> Ac vim quandam naturam naturatam nostri vocant rebus omnibus indidit qua cuncta fierent crescerentque. Hanc Pana appellarunt ab eo quod est pan idest omne, quia die iussu omnia idem sunt propter quam rem naturae deum illum dixerunt.

<cite>Note 262, p. 144 — quoting Cristoforo Landino, Ad Ecl. 2,31</cite>

Landino himself cites Eusebius (*Praeparatio evangelica* 3,11,43) to the effect that "Pana universi symbolum obtinere." Campanini quotes Giorgio da Trebisonda's (Trapezunzio's) Latin translation of Eusebius: "Universi symbolum Pana esse affirmant, cui cornua dederunt propter solem et lunam, variam Pantherae pellem propter varietatem caelestium" (note 265). This Eusebian tradition was central to Zorzi's strategy of baptizing pagan cosmological symbolism into a Christian-Kabbalistic framework.

Note 260 records Plutarch's report (*De defectu oraculorum* 419C) of the announcement of the death of the Great Pan at the time of Christ's passion — a report also cited by Zorzi in *De harmonia mundi* 2,2,16 and in the *Problemata* (6,7,256). The Tiberian imperial archive supposedly authenticated the sailors' testimony that "magnum Pana mortuum esse." Campanini notes that a divergent interpretation appears in *Problemata* 5,4,347, where Pan's name is linked to Socrates' deathbed prayer in Plato's *Phaedrus* (279b).

Francis Bacon's *De sapientia veterum*, cap. VII ("Pan sive natura") is cited (note 258, with reference to Lemmi 1933), and Goethe's *Faust II* is adduced as a late witness to the same tradition: "Das All der Welt / wird vorgestellt / im großen Pan" (note 259). Campanini also cites Lina Bolzoni's work (1991) for a Renaissance memory-theatre description in which Pan's body maps three worlds — supercelestial, celestial, and sublunar (note 270).

## Echo, Creation by Divine Breath, and the Pythagorean Symbolon

Note 268 documents Zorzi's return to the theme of Echo in the *Problemata* (5,5,434), where Zorzi comments on the Pythagorean golden verse enjoining worship of Echo when the wind blows. Zorzi interprets Echo as the creature's reverberation back to God:

> Cuius rei (ni fallor) mysterium est, quod creatura non solum facta est voce Dei iubenti, sed exivit ab eo tamquam vox vel flatus. Hinc antiqui Theologi dicunt quod Deus fecit omnem creaturam per (he), quod est nota aspirationis, eo quod spiritu, flatu et voce omnia produxit. Tunc autem redit vox flante vento, quando gratia Spiritus sancti perflantis reflectitur in Deum, quantum potest.

<cite>Note 268, p. 144–145 — quoting Zorzi, Problemata 5,5,434</cite>

The Hebrew letter *he* (ה), described as "nota aspirationis," is here the instrument of divine creation — a distinctly kabbalistic pneumatology in which breath, spirit, and voice are identified. This connects the Pythagorean musical-harmonic tradition with the Hebrew linguistic-cosmogonic one.

## The Hermetic Sphere: God as Infinite Center

Note 269 cites *Problemata* f. 322v (5,3,296) for Zorzi's use of the Hermetic formula of God as "sphaera intelligibilis cuius centrum est ubique, circumferentia vero nusquam" — a commonplace derived from the pseudo-Hermetic *Liber XXIV philosophorum* and widely diffused through Ficino and Cusanus. Zorzi's gloss is cosmological: all created things bearing the vestige of God tend toward roundness (heavens, earth, elements), while God's circumference is nowhere because He is unconditioned by place.

## The Tetragrammaton and Christian Kabbalah

Notes 272–278 constitute the most theologically dense portion of the chunk, addressing Zorzi's use of the four-letter divine name (יהוה). Campanini cites Ficino's *De christiana religione* (Opera, Basel 1576, p. 1309; also the Venice 1491 edition at Uppsala), noting that a reader of the Uppsala copy added a Hebrew Tetragrammaton in the margin next to the passage in question. Ficino argues that the divine names were scattered and veiled in ancient writings, but that the first and greatest name — composed of four letters, all vowels — can only be correctly pronounced by one divinely inspired, and that this compels acknowledgment that Jesus the Nazarene was divine.

Campanini then cites *De harmonia mundi* 1,3,16, where Zorzi declares:

> Sunt igitur fundamenta haec omnium rerum quadrifaria a Deo, cui est nomen quatuor literarum, quarum quaelibet circularem numerum continet, et hinc omnes numeros, qui circulares dicuntur, illae literae complectuntur, unde magno mysterio nobis innuitur quod omnis revolutio generationis et corruptionis et vicissitudinis rerum reverti tandem debet in illud a quo processerunt.

<cite>Note 273, p. 146 — quoting De harmonia mundi 1,3,16</cite>

The fourfold name thus encodes cyclical number and the return of all things to their origin — the harmonic principle of *conversio* that organizes the entire *De harmonia mundi*.

Notes 275–278 introduce an anonymous medieval Christian-Jewish dialogue preserved in *Patrologia Latina* CLVII, cols. 535–672 (noted in Williams 1935 and Büchler 1986). From col. 611, Campanini quotes a remarkable passage in which the Tetragrammaton (יהוה, rendered in corrupted font as encoded characters) is shown to contain the Trinity: the four letters combine pairwise into three distinct divine names, each pair yielding one Person, while the full four letters yield the one divine name and thus the unity of substance:

> Si tamen attendas subtilius, et illud Dei nomen, quod in secretis secretorum explanatum invenitur [...] nomen inquam trium litterarum, quamvis quatuor figuris [...] videbis quia idem nomen et unum sit et tria: scilicet quod unum ad unitatem substantiae, quod vero tria, ad Trinitatem respicit personarum.

<cite>Note 277, p. 146 — quoting PL CLVII, col. 611</cite>

This is the locus classicus for the Christian-kabbalistic reading of the Tetragrammaton as Trinitarian proof, a tradition Zorzi deployed systematically in *De harmonia mundi*. Note 278 indicates that the diagram supporting this analysis is reproduced in Williams (1935, p. 236).

## Editorial Significance

These notes reveal Campanini's scholarly method: he cross-references Zorzi's sources across *De harmonia mundi*, the *Problemata*, and external humanist works, demonstrating that Zorzi's harmonic cosmology was not merely eclectic but systematically coordinated across the pagan cosmological allegory of Pan, Pythagorean and Platonic philosophy, Hermetic theology, and Christian Kabbalah centered on the Hebrew divine name. The chunk is thus a bibliographic map of the intellectual substrate beneath Zorzi's central claim that all creation participates in a divinely structured harmony returning to its Tetragrammatic source.
